content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 1 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 265-266 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_001_page_001.jpg, 1PE_001_page_002.jpg

1. Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ, to the elect sojourners of the dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia,

2. according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, in the sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ: Grace to you and peace be multiplied.

3. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who according to his great mercy begat us again unto a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead,

4. unto an inheritance incorruptible and undefiled and unfading, kept in heaven for you,

5. who by the power of God are being guarded through faith unto a salvation ready to be revealed in the last time.

6. In this you rejoice greatly, though now for a little while, if it must be, you have been grieved by various trials,

7. so that the tested genuineness of your faith — more precious than gold that perishes though tested by fire — may be found unto praise and glory and honor at the revelation of Jesus Christ.

8. Whom, not having seen, you love; in whom, though you do not now see him, yet believing, you rejoice with joy inexpressible and filled with glory,

9. receiving the outcome of your faith — the salvation of your souls.

10. Concerning this salvation, the prophets who prophesied about the grace that was to come to you searched and inquired diligently,

11. searching into what person or what time the Spirit of Christ within them was indicating when it testified beforehand the sufferings destined for Christ and the glories to follow them.

12. To whom it was revealed that not to themselves but to you they were ministering these things, which have now been announced to you through those who proclaimed the gospel to you by the Holy Spirit sent from heaven — things into which angels desire to look.

13. Therefore, girding up the loins of your mind, being sober, set your hope fully upon the grace being brought to you at the revelation of Jesus Christ.

14. As children of obedience, do not be conformed to the former desires of your ignorance,

15. but as he who called you is holy, you also become holy in all your conduct,

16. because it is written, "You shall be holy, for I am holy."

17. And if you call upon the Father who judges impartially according to each one's work, conduct yourselves in fear during the time of your sojourning,

18. knowing that you were ransomed from the futile conduct handed down from your fathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold,

19. but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot,

20. who was foreknown indeed before the foundation of the world but was made manifest in the last times for your sake,

21. who through him are believers in God, who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, so that your faith and hope are in God.

22. Having purified your souls by your obedience to the truth unto genuine brotherly love, love one another earnestly from the heart,

23. having been born again, not of perishable seed but of imperishable, through the living and abiding word of God.

24. For "all flesh is as grass, and all its glory as the flower of grass. The grass withered, and the flower fell away,

25. but the word of the Lord abides forever." And this is the word that was proclaimed to you as good news.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Elect sojourners of the dispersion" (v.1):** ἐκλεκτοῖς παρεπιδήμοις διασπορᾶς — Peter addresses his audience with a striking fusion of Jewish and Christian identity. "Dispersion" (diaspora) was the standard term for Jews scattered outside Palestine; "sojourners" (parepidēmoi) denotes temporary residents in a foreign land. Applied to Gentile-inclusive Christian communities in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor, these terms redefine the people of God as spiritual exiles whose true homeland is heavenly. The five provinces listed roughly trace a circular route through northern and western Anatolia.

2. **Trinitarian salutation (v.2):** The greeting is structured around the work of all three persons: the Father's foreknowledge, the Spirit's sanctification, and Christ's blood. "Sprinkling of blood" (ῥαντισμὸν αἵματος) alludes to the covenant ratification at Sinai (Exodus 24:3-8), where Moses sprinkled blood on the people to seal the covenant.

3. **"Begat us again" (v.3):** ἀναγεννήσας — Sinaiticus reads this as an aorist participle. The verb is rare, appearing in the New Testament only here and in v.23 (in passive form). Peter grounds regeneration not in human decision but in divine mercy and the historical event of Christ's resurrection. This regeneration produces a "living hope" (ἐλπίδα ζῶσαν) — hope that is itself alive because its object, Christ, is risen.

4. **The inheritance described by three alpha-privative adjectives (v.4):** ἄφθαρτον καὶ ἀμίαντον καὶ ἀμάραντον — "incorruptible and undefiled and unfading." Each negation contrasts with the earthly inheritance of Israel: the land could be ravaged, the temple defiled, the glory diminished. The heavenly inheritance suffers none of these. The rare ἀμάραντον ("unfading") may allude to the amaranth flower, proverbially immortal.

5. **"Tested genuineness of your faith" (v.7):** τὸ δοκίμιον ὑμῶν τῆς πίστεως — the noun δοκίμιον denotes the proven or tested quality, not the process of testing itself. The gold-and-fire analogy is drawn from Proverbs 17:3 and Wisdom 3:5-6. The paradox is that faith, which is imperishable, is more precious than gold precisely because gold perishes even when refined.

6. **"Joy inexpressible and filled with glory" (v.8):** χαρᾷ ἀνεκλαλήτῳ καὶ δεδοξασμένῃ — the perfect passive participle δεδοξασμένῃ ("having been glorified") is remarkable. This is not merely future eschatological joy but present joy that already participates in heavenly glory. The compound ἀνεκλαλήτῳ ("unutterable") occurs only here in the New Testament.

7. **The prophets' search (vv.10-12):** Peter presents an extraordinary theology of prophetic inspiration. The prophets themselves did not fully understand their own oracles — they searched (ἐξεζήτησαν) and inquired (ἐξηραύνησαν) into the meaning of what the Spirit of Christ within them was revealing. The revelation was ultimately "for you" (εἰς ὑμᾶς), the present recipients of the gospel. Even angels remain outsiders to this mystery, desiring merely to "look into" (παρακύψαι) it.

8. **"Girding up the loins of your mind" (v.13):** ἀναζωσάμενοι τὰς ὀσφύας τῆς διανοίας ὑμῶν — a metaphor drawn from the Exodus (Exodus 12:11), where Israel ate the Passover with loins girded for departure. Peter applies this to the mind: intellectual and spiritual readiness for the journey of faith. The middle voice (ἀναζωσάμενοι) implies personal responsibility in this preparation.

9. **"Children of obedience" (v.14):** τέκνα ὑπακοῆς — a Semitic idiom (cf. "sons of disobedience" in Ephesians 2:2) meaning "those characterized by obedience." The formal equivalence preserves the Hebrew-flavored construction. Their identity as obedient children stands in contrast to the former desires that shaped them during their ignorance.

10. **"Futile conduct handed down from your fathers" (v.18):** τῆς ματαίας ὑμῶν ἀναστροφῆς πατροπαραδότου — the adjective πατροπαράδοτος ("handed down from fathers") occurs only here in the New Testament. For Gentile converts, this refers to inherited pagan religious practices. The ransom price is stated in deliberately economic terms — "not with perishable things such as silver or gold" — before the theological price is named: the precious blood of Christ.

11. **"Foreknown before the foundation of the world" (v.20):** προεγνωσμένου μὲν πρὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου — the redemptive plan is not a divine afterthought. Christ's sacrificial role was determined before creation itself. The contrast between "foreknown before" (πρό) and "made manifest in the last times" (ἐπ᾽ ἐσχάτου τῶν χρόνων) frames all of history as the outworking of this eternal purpose.

12. **"Born again of imperishable seed" (v.23):** ἀναγεγεννημένοι ... διὰ λόγου ζῶντος θεοῦ καὶ μένοντος — returning to the theme of v.3, Peter now specifies the instrument of regeneration: the living and abiding word of God. The Isaiah quotation (40:6-8) that follows in vv.24-25 provides the scriptural warrant — all flesh is transient, but God's word endures. Peter then identifies this enduring word with the gospel proclaimed to his readers.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
